coredata.dc:title: Nanozyme-based sensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection platform for glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Qingjie
Zhou, Xiaobin
Wang, Mengjun
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: Development and application of carbon-based nanozymes are attracting wide interest in recent years. Abnormal glucose level can be threat to human health. Sensitive and accurate sensing methods for glucose are still of great urgency. In this work, we constructed a sensitive nanozyme-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for glucose. The sensing system composed of copper-doped carbon-based nanozyme (CuAA) with superb peroxidase-like activity and Mg/N doped carbon quantum dots (Mg–N-CQDs) with distinguished fluorescence property. Efficient tandem catalysis of glucose oxidase (Glu Ox) and CuAA, and inner-filter effect (IFE) between 2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP) and Mg–N-CQDs played crucial roles in this sensing system. The oxidization of glucose was catalyzed by Glu Ox firstly to produce H2O2. In the presence of H2O2, fluorescent DAP was formed from non-fluorescent substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD) with assistance of CuAA, resulting in generation of emission at 558 nm. Meanwhile, the emission at 444 nm from Mg–N-CQDs was quenched efficiently by DAP through IFE. The ratiometric fluorescence signal I558/I444 increased linearly with glucose concentration in the scope of 2–400 μmol L−1, the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.56 μmol L−1. It was also practicable to apply the method to the determination of glucose in human serum, satisfactory recoveries and RSDs were acquired.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-based nanozyme
Tandem catalysis
Ratiometric fluorescence
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of glucose in serum sample
Characterization for nanozyme CuAA and Mg–N-CQDs
Investigation of paroxidase-like activity of CuAA
Construction of detection system and mechanism clarification
Detection of glucose in real sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775052 ), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20180414013GH ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of precursor and CuAA (A) TEM image of precursor CuNS; (B), (C) SEM images of CuAA; (D) TEM image of CuAA; (E) XRD pattern of CuAA.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of OPD, OPD/H2O2, OPD/CuAA and OPD/H2O2/CuAA system, respectively. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of OPD, OPD/H2O2, OPD/CuAA and OPD/H2O2/CuAA system, respectively. (C) Absorbance before and after inhibition by scavengers.

(A) Michaelis-Menten curve under 1 mM H2O2 with various OPD concentrations, (B) Lineweaver - Burk plot for OPD. (C) Michaelis-Menten curve under 0.3 mM OPD and various H2O2 concentrations, (D) Lineweaver−Burk plot for H2O2.

(A) Absorption for DAP and fluorescence spectrum for Mg–N-CQDs. (B) Fluorescence decay traces of Mg–N-CQDs and Mg–N-CQDs/DAP system. (C) Fluorescence spectra of Mg–N-CQDs with blank, OPD, H2O2, CuAA, OPD/CuAA, H2O2/CuAA, OPD/H2O2, OPD/H2O2/CuAA, glucose, glucose oxidase, glucose/OPD/CuAA, glucose oxidase/OPD/CuAA, glucose/glucose oxidase/OPD/CuAA system, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence spectra variation of OPD/CuAA/Mg–N-CQDs system with H2O2 concentrations (5–600 μM). (B) Linear connection between response signal F558/F444 and H2O2 concentration. (C) Fluorescence spectra variation of OPD/CuAA/Glu Ox/Mg–N-CQDs system with glucose concentration (2–400 μM). (D) Linear connection between fluorescence ratio F558/F444 and glucose concentrations.

(A) Fluorescence response of OPD/CuAA/Glu Ox/Mg–N-CQDs system with different species. Concentration: 600 μM for glucose, 2.5 mM for cholesterol, 5 mM for the others. (B) Fluorescence sensing system responses to glucose in the presence of various species. Concentration: 600 μM for glucose, 625 μM for cholesterol, 1.25 mM for the others. The concentration of Glu Ox is 500 U L−1 for both (A) and (B).
(A) Illustration of synthesis process for CuAA. (B) Illustration of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for glucose.
Determination of glucose in human serum.

head.ce:title: Nanozyme-based sensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection platform for glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development and application of carbon-based nanozymes are attracting wide interest in recent years. Abnormal glucose level can be threat to human health. Sensitive and accurate sensing methods for glucose are still of great urgency. In this work, we constructed a sensitive nanozyme-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for glucose. The sensing system composed of copper-doped carbon-based nanozyme (CuAA) with superb peroxidase-like activity and Mg/N doped carbon quantum dots (Mg–N-CQDs) with distinguished fluorescence property. Efficient tandem catalysis of glucose oxidase (Glu Ox) and CuAA, and inner-filter effect (IFE) between 2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP) and Mg–N-CQDs played crucial roles in this sensing system. The oxidization of glucose was catalyzed by Glu Ox firstly to produce H2O2. In the presence of H2O2, fluorescent DAP was formed from non-fluorescent substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD) with assistance of CuAA, resulting in generation of emission at 558 nm. Meanwhile, the emission at 444 nm from Mg–N-CQDs was quenched efficiently by DAP through IFE. The ratiometric fluorescence signal I558/I444 increased linearly with glucose concentration in the scope of 2–400 μmol L−1, the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.56 μmol L−1. It was also practicable to apply the method to the determination of glucose in human serum, satisfactory recoveries and RSDs were acquired.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzymes are catalysts that convert substrates into products in biological environment, usually with protein or RNA structure. However, enzymes tend to lose activity under harsh environment like extreme pH and high temperature. Various artificial enzyme mimetics including polymers, cyclodextrins and supramolecular compounds, have been developed to overcome these defects [1]. Since Yan's team found out that magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles possess inbred peroxidase-like performance [2], new generation of enzyme mimics termed as nanozymes attracts great interest for merits like low cost, stability and easy preparation. And high performance nanozymes are applied in various fields, like biosensing, tumor treatment, antibacterial, etc. Up to now, various nano-materials, including noble metal nanoparticles [3], transition metal oxide-based [4–6] and carbon-based materials [7–9], have been developed to mimic peroxidase, oxidase [10,11], catalase [12,13], etc. Among them, carbon-based nanozymes exhibit superior catalytic performance owning to their unique geometric and electronic configurations [14]. Transition metal-Nx active sites have been widely designed in nanozymes to mimic active sites of natural enzyme [15–18]. But metal atoms tend to aggregate during pyrolysis [19]. So that, introducing high nitrogen containing carbon substrate to fix metal atoms and form the metal-Nx site is much helpful [20]. In this work, we synthesized a high-performance peroxidase-like nanozyme CuAA by adopting high nitrogen containing 2D sheet-like g-C3N4 as substrate; the large surface area of 2D g-C3N4 is additionally favorable for the exposure of active sites and the improvement of catalytic performance [19]. Further, CuAA was successfully applied to glucose sensing.

Glucose is an essential energy providing nutrient in human body metabolism activity. Aberrant blood glucose level can result in various diseases, including diabetes mellitus, cardiovascular disease, kidney failure, etc [21,22]. In this case, an efficient and practical method to determine and monitor blood glucose level is still urgently needed [23]. Until now, various detection methodologies have been developed for the detection of glucose, including surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [21], electrochemical analysis [22], electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL) [24], etc. But these methods are time-consuming or high-cost. As a fast and convenient detection method, fluorescence sensing is attracting great interest. However, they are usually carried out by measuring single fluorescence signal [25,26]. The interference from performance variation of apparatus, environment and background cannot be eliminated. Ratiometric fluorescence sensing can avoid these drawbacks effectually through self-calibration [27–29]. Therefore, we introduced Mg/N doped CQDs (Mg–N-CQDs) with exactly matched fluorescence emission as inner calibration, which largely improved the sensitivity and accuracy of the sensing method.

Herein, a copper-doped carbon-based nanozyme CuAA bearing superb peroxidase-like activity was synthesized and applied to accurate determination of glucose. The sensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection system for glucose was established based on the tandem catalysis of Glucose Oxidase (Glu Ox) and CuAA, and effective inner-filter effect (IFE) between fluorescent DAP and Mg–N-CQDs. Just like shown in Scheme 1 B, Glu Ox catalyzed the redox reaction between glucose and O2 to yield H2O2. Then CuAA catalyzed the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to produce 2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with emission of 558 nm. DAP further quenched the fluorescence of Mg–N-CQDs at 444 nm through IFE. Therefore, the increase of emission at 558 nm and decrease at 444 nm could be observed, and the fluorescence ratio signal I558/I444 could be monitored to quantitatively detect glucose.
Details about reagents and apparatus have been presented in Supporting Information.
CuAA was synthesized according to previous report with some modification [30,31].

In detail, Cu (NO3)2 ⋅3H2O (1.2 g) and L-AA (2.4 g) were dispersed in 360 mL pure water and stirred for minutes under ambient temperature. Thereafter CTAB (2.4 g) and HMTA (2.4 g) were added, followed by stirring for 30 min to form a milky suspension. Then the suspension was heated to 80 °C and kept for 3 h. After cooling down to ambient temperature, precursor CuNS was attained through centrifugation and washing with mixture of water/ethyl alcohol (volume ratio = 1:1). Finally it was dried at 75 °C for 2 h.
g-C3N4 was synthesized through annealing melamine at 550 °C for 2 h according to previous report with modification [32,33].

Subsequently, CuNS mixed with g-C3N4 at 1:10 mass ratio was totally milled. Then the mixture was annealed in N2 ambiance at 800 °C for 2 h. At last, the pyrolyzed powder got etched in HNO3 for 5 h at 80 °C to obtain CuAA. Details have been presented in Scheme 1A.

Mg–N-CQDs were synthesized referring to previous report with certain modification [34]. Briefly, 0.5 mL ethylene diamine was gradually added into 16 mL aqueous solution of citric acid (0.4 g) and MgCO3 (2.4 g). After that, the mixture was heated at 200 °C for 3 h in 25 mL autoclave. Then collect yellow product through centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 30 min after cooling to ambient temperature. Finally, to get purified, raw product was dialyzed in DI water for 24 h. The final product presents as white flake.

Evaluation of peroxidase-like performance for CuAA was processed as following. OPD (0.04 mL, 4 mM), acetate buffer (0.2 mL, pH 4.0), H2O2 (0.08 mL, 10 mM) were mixed firstly, then water and 0.064 mL CuAA (0.1 mg mL−1) were added to final volume of 0.8 mL. After incubation at 45 °C for 35 min, fluorescence and US-vis absorption were both measured. The catalytic mechanism was investigated similarly, except for the addition of corresponding scavengers.

Kinetic analysis of CuAA was conducted as following. Briefly, for the substrate OPD, 0.05 mL OPD with concentrations ranging from 1 mM to 10 mM, acetate buffer (0.25 mL, pH 4.0), H2O2 (0.1 mL, 10 mM) and H2O (0.52 mL) were mixed under 45 °C, then 0.08 mL CuAA (0.1 mg mL−1) was added to above mixture, the UV–vis absorbance was recorded with an interval of 1 min. For the substrate H2O2, the concentration of OPD was fixed while changing the concentration of H2O2.

To detect H2O2, OPD (0.05 mL, 4 mM), acetate buffer (0.25 mL, pH 4.0),0.1 mL H2O2 with concentrations ranging from 0.05 to 6 mM and H2O (0.02 mL) were firstly mixed, then 0.08 mL CuAA (0.1 mg mL−1) was added. After cultivation in water bath of 45 °C for 35 min, Tris-HCl solution (0.35 mL, pH 8.0) and Mg–N-CQDs (0.15 mL, 30 μg mL−1) were added in sequence. Then, the fluorescence intensity was recorded with excitation of 360 nm.

To detect glucose, mixture of 0.05 mL glucose with various concentrations, acetate buffer (0.025 mL, pH 7.0) and Glu Ox (0.025 mL, 20 U mL−1) was cultivated in water bath of 37 °C for 20 min to produce H2O2; Then OPD (0.05 mL, 4 mM), acetate buffer (0.25 mL, pH 4.0), H2O (0.02 mL) and 0.08 mL CuAA were added. The following procedure was the same as the H2O2 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of glucose in serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: Before spiked with glucose of concentrations of 100, 200, 300 μΜ, human serum was pretreated through centrifugation for 20 min and diluted by 100 times to get rid of potential interference; Fluorescence spectra were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization for nanozyme CuAA and Mg–N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image (Fig. 1 A) displayed that the precursor CuNS was a triangle-like plane, which had large specific surface area. The large specific surface area was beneficial for the contact with surrounding substances and release of Cu atom [30]. The SEM image (Fig. 1B) of CuAA showed its curving 2D sheet-like morphology with smooth surface. That could be attributed to the decomposing of triangle-like plane and the binding of Cu on large area g-C3N4 sheet after pyrolysis. Fig. 1C showed large amount of loosely packed thin sheets in CuAA, thus giving out large surface area. The large surface area is beneficial for affinity to substrates. As seen in TEM image (Fig. 1D), the 2D nanosheet was transparent, demonstrating that the nanosheet of CuAA is very thin, conducive to satisfying dispersity in water and electron transfer [35]. As observed in Fig. 1E, typical peaks of 24° and 44° appeared in the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CuAA. They could be attributed to lattice planes of (002) and (100) in N-doped graphite [36], which further indicated that CuAA was carbonized to graphitic carbon after pyrolysis. No inspected crystalline copper bulk species were in XRD pattern, proving that no obvious aggregation occurred during pyrolysis [37].

In XRD pattern (Fig. S1A) of Mg–N-CQDs, the broad peak centered at 24° illustrated that it was amorphous carbon with some characters of monoclinic caffeine hydrate (C8H10N4O2 ⋅ H2O) (JCPDS card no. 31–1570) [38]. In IR spectrum of Mg–N-CQDs (Fig. S1B), there were double peaks of 3378 and 3588 cm−1, which ascribed to the characteristic absorbance of –NH2 [39]. Stretching vibration of –COO− corresponded to the absorptions at 1642 and 1408 cm−1; the peak of 1250 cm−1 corresponding to C–N stretching vibration could be also found [34,40]. As presented in Figs. S1C and 357 nm and 444 nm were the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of Mg–N-CQDs. For convenience, following fluorescent measurements were excited at 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of paroxidase-like activity of CuAA
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like property of CuAA was verified and presented in Fig. 2 A and B. Under identical condition, the fluorescence intensity and absorbance increased largely only with the coexistence of H2O2 and CuAA. H2O2 alone barely induced distinct fluorescence or absorbance change, which indicated that H2O2 could not oxidase OPD to DAP without the assistance of CuAA. CuAA alone could not lead to obvious spectra change, indicating that the oxidase-like property of CuAA is negligible compared with its peroxidase-like property. Therefor the exclusive peroxidase-like behavior of CuAA was well proved.

The possible catalysis mechanism was that H2O2 adsorbed on the surface of CuAA, peroxidase-like CuAA then catalyzed the reduction of H2O2 to produce reactive oxide species (ROS), ROS subsequently oxidized OPD to DAP. Different ROS scavengers such as p-BQ for O2 •-, propanol for •OH, and NaN3 for 1O2 were introduced to the OPD/H2O2/CuAA system to confirm the exact ROS species transformed from H2O2. As seen in Fig. 2C, OPD/H2O2/CuAA system had absorption at 450 nm. Scavenger propanol had no obvious effect on the absorbance, which excluded the existence of •OH. On the contrary, the addition of p-BQ and NaN3 induced distinct absorbance decline, which verified the existence of O2 •- and 1O2. And O2 •- was the majority, because less amount of p-BQ led to larger absorbance decline. Therefore, it was reasonable to confirm that H2O2 is firstly transformed to O2 •-, then O2 •- oxidize colorless substrate OPD to yellow-colored DAP.

Owning to the pH effect on catalytic activity of nanozymes, the optimal pH value was investigated. As presented in Fig. S2, the highest absorbance appeared at pH 4 and kept consistent when pH was less than 4.0. So that steady-state kinetics parameters Km and Vm were assessed under condition of pH 4.0.

Steady-state kinetics parameters were acquired according to Michaelis-Menten equation V = Vm × [S]/(Km+[S]), in which V, Vm, [S] and Km depict the initial reaction rate, maximal reaction rate, concentration of OPD or H2O2 and Michaelis constant, respectively [41,42]. Concentration of DAP (c) with maximum absorption at 446 nm (ε = 2.1 × 104 M− 1 cm− 1) [43] was monitored on UV–vis spectrophotometer according to Beer-Lambert Law c = A/(ε b). V equals the initial slope of plot c – time. Thus typical Michaelis-Menten curves V – [S] toward H2O2 and OPD were acquired (Fig. 3 A and C). When velocity data were fitted to Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal equation (Fig. 3B and D), which derives from Michaelis-Menten equation, Km and Vm were figured out. Km for OPD and H2O2 were 0.18 and 0.047 mM respectively; Vm for OPD and H2O2 were 14.1 × 10−8 and 3.83 × 10−8 M s−1 respectively. Lower Km stands for the better affinity of nanozymes for according substrate, and higher Vm reflects better catalytic ability for substrate [44,45]. As shown in Table S1, Km for OPD is lower than most listed nanozymes, suggesting that CuAA has satisfactory affinity for OPD; Km for H2O2 is much lower than listed nanozymes, identifying the excellent affinity of CuAA for H2O2. Vm for H2O2 and OPD are higher than most reported nanozymes. These outstanding constants confirm that CuAA is a high-performance nanozyme. Compared to natural peroxidase HRP, whose Km was measured as 0.59 mM for OPD and 0.34 mM for H2O2 respectively, Vm as 4.65 × 10−8 M s−1 for OPD and 9.48 × 10−8 M s−1 for H2O2, respectively [46], CuAA has superior performance. Therefore, CuAA has potential to be an alternative for HRP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of detection system and mechanism clarification
ce:section.ce:para: Like in Fig. 4 A, absorption spectrum of DAP overlapped well with emission spectrum of Mg–N-CQDs. According to the literature [47], the overlapping happens in the process of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or IFE. But FRET can induce the decline of lifetime of CQDs. However, there was no lifetime variation of Mg–N-CQDs before and after the addition of DAP (Fig. 4B), which indicated that IFE dominates the quenching process of Mg–N-CQDs. Peroxidase-like nanozymes are widely applied to determination of H2O2 and H2O2 related analytes. As shown in Scheme 1B, H2O2 absorbed firstly on the surface of CuAA, then converted to ROS O2 •- with the catalysis of CuAA. O2 •- subsequently oxidized OPD to fluorescent DAP. Thus fluorescence at 558 nm emerged once H2O2 was added. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of Mg–N-CQDs at 444 nm was quenched. In this way, ratiometric fluorescence signal I558/I444 could be monitored to determine H2O2. Since Glu Ox is capable to selectively oxidize glucose and produce H2O2, the sensing platform can be used to detect glucose quantitatively. Feasibility was studied and shown in Fig. 4C. For Mg–N-CQDs/OPD/CuAA system, only the presence of H2O2 or Glu/Glu Ox could induce distinct increase of I558/I444, the presence of glucose or Glu Ox alone could not lead to obvious ratiometric fluorescence signal (I558/I444) increase. Above all, a sensitive platform for detection of H2O2 and glucose was constructed.

To improve sensing performance of the proposed detection method, several factors were considered and optimized. In the OPD/H2O2/CuAA/Mg–N-CQDs system, parameters effecting catalytic activity of CuAA were firstly studied. Considering the significant effect of buffer pH, a range of pH from 3 to 7 was examined. As presented in Fig. S3A, best performance appeared under pH 4.0. Therefore, pH 4.0 was selected for following detection. As seen in Fig. S3B, fluorescence signal I558/I444 changed slightly from 40 °C to 50 °C, thus 45 °C was chosen for further experiment. We also investigated CuAA dosage and response time. As exhibited in Fig. S3 C and D, fluorescence signal I558/I444 reached plateau under 8 μg mL−1 of CuAA and reaction time of 35 min. In the OPD/Glu/Glu Ox/CuAA/Mg–N-CQDs system, the Glu Ox concentration and reaction time for oxidization of glucose were optimized. As seen from Fig. S3E, after Glu Ox amounted to 400 U L−1, the fluorescence signal I558/I444 varied slightly with increasing Glu Ox concentration. Finally, 500 U L−1 was chosen in further study. As shown in Fig. S3F, the oxidation of glucose completed 15 min later, and 20 min was picked for following determination.

Under optimal sensing conditions, H2O2 and glucose were determined. Sensitive detection of H2O2 was the prerequisite and basic of detection of glucose. It can be observed from Fig. 5 A that with the increment of H2O2 concentration, the fluorescence (FL) intensity of H2O2/OPD/CuAA/Mg–N-CQDs system increased at 558 nm but decreased at 444 nm. I558/I444 increased linearly as the concentration of H2O2 ranged from 5 to 600 μΜ, as Fig. 5B presented. The linear formula can be fitted as I558/I444 = 0.0068 [H2O2] + 0.394 (R2 = 0.990) with limit of detection (LOD) as 0.98 μΜ according to the rule 3σ/slope. As Fig. 5C presented, with the increment of glucose amount, the FL intensity of OPD/CuAA/Glu/Glu Ox/Mg–N-CQDs system at 558 nm and 444 nm also changed reversely. I558/I444 increased linearly when the amount of glucose ranged from 2 to 400 μΜ as in Fig. 5D. Corresponding linear function can be fitted as I558/I444 = 0.0052 [Glu] + 0.241 (R2 = 0.993). LOD was determined as 1.56 μΜ. The repeatability of this sensing method for glucose was evaluated by relative standard deviation (RSD) at concentration of 100 μM glucose in four replicates, the RSD was 1.07%. The detection performance was compared with other glucose sensing strategies with respect to linearity range and LOD, and listed in Table S2. It was found that the CuAA-based strategy for glucose sensing has wider linearity range and lower LOD.

Selectivity of the detection method for glucose was estimated by using following potential interference: fructose, sucrose, galactose, glycine, histidine, cholesterol, Mg2+, Na+, Ca2+, NH4 +, K+. As observed in Fig. 6 A, no obvious ratiometric fluorescence signal increase was discerned after addition of these species even with higher concentration than glucose. On the contrary, only glucose could lead to distinct increase of the ratiometric fluorescence signal, which demonstrated that the proposed sensing method has satisfying selectivity for glucose. Anti-interference assay was conducted and presented in Fig. 6B. When interferents coexisted with glucose, the ratiometric fluorescence signal had no obvious changes compared with glucose present alone. Thus it was demonstrated that the glucose sensing platform possesses good enough anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of the CuAA-based platform for glucose sensing was proceeded in human serum and presented in Table 1 . Recovery experiment was taken by adding 100, 200, 300 μΜ of glucose into serum sample. As seen in Table 1, recoveries ranged from 100.52% to 107.41%, relative standard deviations (RSDs) were no higher than 1.31%. They verified the good accuracy of this nanozyme based ratiometric fluorescence sensing method for glucose. Therefore it was manifested that the sensing method for glucose is practical in actual determination.

Although the established method for glucose sensing showed outstanding performance, it also has some limitations, there is still much room for the elevation of catalytic activity of nanozyme, and the efficiency of tandem catalysis can also be improved by the integration of two enzymes in one material.

In summary, carbon-based nanozyme CuAA was successfully synthesized by using g-C3N4 as carbon support and nitrogen provider, and its application in glucose sensing was explored. Its peroxidase-like property was estimated and its catalytic ability was determined to be superior to most nanozymes. By utilizing the cascade catalysis reaction of glucose oxidase and CuAA, and IFE between catalytic product DAP and Mg/N co-doped CQDs, a sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy for glucose had been established. The application in determination of glucose in actual sample also acquired satisfying recoveries and RSDs. Hence, the detection strategy is of practical meaning for sensitive and accurate determination of glucose.

Qingjie Fu: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft, preparation. Xiaobin Zhou: Data curation, Resources. Mengjun Wang: Data curation. Xingguang Su: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775052), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20180414013GH).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2022.339993.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanozymes: classification, catalytic mechanisms, activity regulation, and applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of ferromagnetic nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Gao, J. Zhuang, L. Nie, J. Zhang, Y. Zhang, N. Gu, T. Wang, J. Feng, D. Yang, S. Perrett, X. Yan, Intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of ferromagnetic nanoparticles, Nat. Nanotechnol. 2 (2007) 577-583.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot cascade catalysis at neutral pH driven by CuO tandem nanozyme for ascorbic acid and alkaline phosphatase detection

Chen, One-pot cascade catalysis at neutral pH driven by CuO tandem nanozyme for ascorbic acid and alkaline phosphatase detection, Sensor. Actuator. B Chem. 321 (2020) 128511.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ growth of CeO2 on g-C3N4 nanosheets toward a spherical g-C3N4/CeO2 nanozyme with enhanced peroxidase-like catalysis: a selective colorimetric analysis strategy for mercury(II)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-catalyzed surface reaction-induced fluorescence resonance energy transfer on cysteine-stabilized MnO2 quantum dots for selective detection of dopamine
